title: MVP Infrastructure as Code (Bicep)
description:
## Overview

Complete Bicep IaC to provision the entire MVP from scratch. One `./infra/deploy.sh` on a fresh subscription stands up everything and deploys code. No manual Azure Portal clicks required.

## Prerequisites

- Azure CLI installed and logged in (`az login`)
- Subscription with Owner role
- Node.js (for func CLI and swa CLI — auto-installed by deploy.sh if missing)

## deploy.sh — One Script to Rule Them All

```
./infra/deploy.sh              # Deploy everything (infra + backend + frontend)
./infra/deploy.sh infra        # Infrastructure only (Bicep + Key Vault secrets)
./infra/deploy.sh code         # Code only (backend + frontend)
./infra/deploy.sh backend      # Function App code only
./infra/deploy.sh frontend     # Static Web App only
./infra/deploy.sh status       # Show what is deployed
./infra/deploy.sh teardown teardown   # Destroy everything (double confirmation)
```

The script auto-detects what needs doing:
- Checks which resource providers are registered, only registers missing ones
- Purges soft-deleted Key Vault and Cognitive Services resources (blocks redeploy with same names)
- Grants deploying user Key Vault Secrets Officer with retry loop for RBAC propagation
- Links SWA backend to Function App (frontend calls /api/* directly, no CORS needed)

## What Bicep Creates (Everything)

### 1. AI Speech Service
- Name: psr-speech-dev, Kind: SpeechServices
- SKU: F0 (free tier — 5 hrs/mo transcription, covers ~7 sermons)
- Location: eastus2
- Fast transcription API proven in POC #6 (3,762 words in 44s)

### 2. Azure OpenAI Account
- Name: psr-openai-dev, Kind: OpenAI
- SKU: S0 (standard)
- Location: eastus2
- No custom subdomain — existing resource uses regional endpoint. Key-based auth via Key Vault secrets works without one. If managed identity auth is needed later, add custom subdomain then.
- Public network access: Enabled

### 3. Azure OpenAI Model Deployments
- `o4-mini`: model o4-mini, version 2025-04-16, Standard, 80K TPM — Pass 1: Biblical Analysis
- `gpt-41`: model gpt-4.1, version 2025-04-14, Standard, 50K TPM — Pass 2: Structure & Content
- `gpt-41-mini`: model gpt-4.1-mini, version 2025-04-14, Standard, 80K TPM — Pass 3: Delivery + Classification

Note: Model versions may need updating if deploying later. Check `az cognitiveservices model list --location eastus2 --query "[?model.name=='o4-mini']"` for current versions.

### 4. Storage Account (Blob)
- Name: psrstoragedev (globally unique)
- SKU: Standard_LRS, Kind: StorageV2
- Container: sermon-audio (private)
- Also serves as AzureWebJobsStorage for the Function App runtime (no second storage account needed)

### 5. Cosmos DB (Serverless)
- Account: psr-cosmos-dev, API: NoSQL
- Capacity: Serverless ($0 at idle)
- Database: psr
- Container: sermons (partition key: /id) — metadata, transcript, PSR scores, status

### 6. Key Vault
- Name: psr-kv-dev, SKU: Standard
- Access: RBAC (no access policies)
- Secrets auto-populated by deploy.sh:
  - speech-key, speech-endpoint
  - openai-key, openai-endpoint, openai-api-version (2025-01-01-preview — required for o4-mini reasoning model)
  - cosmos-connection-string
  - storage-connection-string

### 7. Log Analytics Workspace
- Name: psr-logs-dev, SKU: PerGB2018, Retention: 30 days
- Required dependency for App Insights

### 8. Application Insights
- Name: psr-insights-dev, Kind: web
- Linked to Log Analytics workspace

### 9. Function App (Python, Consumption)
- Name: psr-functions-dev
- Runtime: Python 3.12, OS: Linux, Plan: Consumption (Y1)
- functionTimeout: 00:10:00 (set in host.json, not Bicep — single source of truth)
- App Settings:
  - AzureWebJobsStorage: direct storage connection string (NOT via Key Vault — runtime needs it at cold start before managed identity is ready)
  - APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING: direct (same reason)
  - WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE: 1 (required for consistent Python deployment on Linux consumption)
  - All others via Key Vault references: SPEECH_KEY, SPEECH_ENDPOINT, OPENAI_KEY, OPENAI_ENDPOINT, OPENAI_API_VERSION, COSMOS_CONNECTION_STRING, STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING
- Managed Identity: System-assigned
- CORS: configured for SWA origin + howwas.church + localhost:3000 (fallback if linked backend not used)

### 10. Static Web App
- Name: psr-web-dev, SKU: Free
- Build: Next.js. Bicep creates resource; deploy.sh deploys code via SWA CLI.
- Linked backend: deploy.sh runs `az staticwebapp backends link` after Bicep to proxy /api/* to Function App. This means the frontend just calls `/api/sermons` — no CORS, no separate Function App URL needed.

### 11. RBAC Assignments (Function App managed identity)
- Key Vault Secrets User on psr-kv-dev
- Storage Blob Data Contributor on psrstoragedev
- Cognitive Services User on psr-openai-dev + psr-speech-dev
- Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor on psr-cosmos-dev

## Bicep File Structure

```
infra/
  main.bicep           — orchestrator, calls all modules
  main.bicepparam      — dev environment parameter values
  modules/
    speech.bicep       — AI Speech service (F0)
    openai.bicep       — OpenAI account + 3 model deployments
    storage.bicep      — storage account + blob container
    cosmos.bicep       — Cosmos DB serverless + database + container
    keyvault.bicep     — Key Vault
    monitoring.bicep   — Log Analytics + App Insights
    functions.bicep    — Function App + consumption plan + settings + CORS
    staticwebapp.bicep — Static Web App (bare resource, backend linked by deploy.sh)
    rbac.bicep         — all managed identity role assignments
  deploy.sh            — one script: infra + code + teardown + status
```

## Cost at Idle: ~$0.51/mo

Storage ~$0.50, everything else $0 (serverless/free tiers). Per-sermon: ~$0.75.
close_reason: Audit complete — all 11 Azure resources deployed and verified against spec. 9 Bicep modules, deploy.sh with all 7 commands, 7 Key Vault secrets, 4 RBAC assignments, 3 OpenAI model deployments. Both child beads (sermon-1rn pipeline, sermon-1qd API) closed. Only minor gap: Cosmos DB uses connection string auth instead of RBAC — acceptable for MVP. Pipeline is processing sermons end-to-end (6 scored sermons in production).

title: Harden deploy.sh — error trap, auto-chain backend after infra, timing, CLI guard
description:
## Deploy Script Improvements

Fresh-eyes review of infra/deploy.sh found 5 actionable improvements + 1 memory gap.

### 1. Auto-chain backend after infra (P0 — prevents known outage)
`deploy.sh infra` wipes Function App code (Bicep redeploy resets siteConfig). This is a known gotcha that causes instant 503. Fix: auto-run `deploy_backend` at the end of `deploy_infra()`, or at minimum print a loud warning.

**Recommendation:** Auto-chain. Change `deploy_infra` to call `deploy_backend` at the end. The `infra` command becomes 'infra + restore backend'. Anyone who truly wants infra-only can Ctrl-C after Bicep completes.

### 2. Error trap with context (P1 — saves debugging time)
`set -euo pipefail` aborts on error but gives no context. Add a trap:
```bash
trap 'echo "✗ Deploy failed at line $LINENO (exit $?)" >&2' ERR
```

### 3. Elapsed time (P2 — quality of life)
Add `SECONDS=0` at top, `echo "Done in ${SECONDS}s"` at bottom. Helps calibrate expectations and spot regressions.

### 4. CLI install fallbacks will always fail (P2 — dead code)
`deploy_backend` and `deploy_frontend` try `npm install -g` for func/swa CLIs, but npm global prefix is `/usr` which requires sudo. These fallbacks silently fail. Replace with clear error messages:
```bash
echo '✗ func CLI not found. Install: sudo npm install -g azure-functions-core-tools@4'
exit 1
```

### 5. Teardown wait timeout (P3 — edge case)
`az group wait --deleted` can hang indefinitely. Add `--timeout 600`.

### 6. Update agent persistent memory (.kiro/memory.md) with deploy script reference
The memory file has basic deploy info but is missing:
- Complete command reference with what each subcommand does
- Which commands are safe to run independently vs which need chaining
- Typical deploy times for each subcommand
- The `APPINSIGHTS_INSTRUMENTATIONKEY` fix (2026-03-07) — Bicep now sets both connection string and legacy key
- The text upload endpoint (`/api/sermons/text`) added 2026-03-06
- Logging config: App Insights sampling excludes Request/Exception/Dependency/Event; host.json updated 2026-03-07

### NOT doing (false alarm from review):
- COSMOS_NAME mismatch — verified it matches. The `-eastus2` suffix in traces is the Cosmos endpoint hostname, not the resource name.
- SWA token caching — not worth the complexity for a 2s call.
- parse_output fragility — only used once, and Bicep outputs are reliable.
close_reason: All 6 items implemented: (1) infra auto-chains backend after Bicep to prevent 503, (2) ERR trap with line number, (3) elapsed time on all commands, (4) dead npm install fallbacks replaced with clear error+exit, (5) teardown wait --timeout 600, (6) memory.md updated with safe-alone status for infra command

title: Configure howwas.church DNS → Azure Static Web App
description:

2. Get the auto-generated hostname (e.g. `xxx.azurestaticapps.net`)
3. Via Porkbun API (keys in .env, API access enabled):
   - Delete ALIAS record (id: 527254244) pointing to `pixie.porkbun.com`
   - Delete wildcard CNAME (id: 527254245) pointing to `pixie.porkbun.com`
   - Add CNAME: `howwas.church` → `xxx.azurestaticapps.net`
4. In Azure Portal → Static Web App → Custom domains → Add `howwas.church`
5. Azure auto-provisions SSL cert
6. Add `www` CNAME redirect if desired

## Current DNS (as of 2026-03-04)
- ALIAS `howwas.church` → `pixie.porkbun.com` (parking page, id: 527254244)
- CNAME `*.howwas.church` → `pixie.porkbun.com` (parking page, id: 527254245)
- NS records (x4) → Porkbun nameservers (keep these)
- Cloudflare: enabled (Porkbun's built-in proxy)

title: API Layer (Azure Functions HTTP triggers)
description:
Three HTTP endpoints the frontend needs, running as Azure Functions:

## POST /api/sermons — Upload

Accept audio upload, validate, kick off processing.

Validation:
- File type: MP3, WAV, M4A only (check Content-Type + magic bytes)
- File size: max 100MB (Azure Functions consumption plan request limit)
- WAV files over 100MB: reject with error suggesting MP3 conversion. Fast transcription API accepts MP3 directly — no need to convert to WAV.
- Duration check: not possible pre-upload without processing. WPM sanity check catches bad files post-transcription.

**Note:** Frontend performs client-side file size and type validation before upload (check file.size and file.type in JS). This prevents users from hitting the Functions 100MB request limit with a confusing server-side error. Backend still validates as a safety net.

Flow:
1. Validate file
2. Create sermon record in Cosmos DB (status: processing, title: user-provided or filename, pastor: user-provided or null, uploadedAt: now)
3. Store audio in Blob Storage (sermon-audio container, key: {id}/{original_filename})
4. Start Durable Functions orchestrator (pass sermon ID + blob URL + user-provided metadata)
5. Return sermon ID immediately

Response: {id, status: processing}

## GET /api/sermons — Feed List

Return all sermons for the feed page. No pagination for MVP.

Response: [{id, title, pastor, date, duration, status, sermonType, compositePsr}]
- status values: processing, complete, failed
- compositePsr is null when status is processing or failed
- title/pastor come from user input (if provided) or LLM metadata extraction (step 4 of pipeline)
- If still processing and no user-provided title, defaults to original filename

## GET /api/sermons/{id} — Full Detail

Return full sermon document from Cosmos DB. Frontend reads: metadata, all 8 category scores + weights + reasoning, composite, strengths/improvements, transcript with segment classifications, radar chart data.

When status is failed:
- error: human-readable error message
- failedAt: ISO 8601 timestamp
- compositePsr, categories, transcript, strengths, improvements: all null

## CORS

Configure for Static Web App origin. In dev: localhost:3000.

## Data Contracts

Defined in docs/frontend-spec.md. The API returns Cosmos DB documents directly — no transformation layer for MVP.

title: Sermon Processing Pipeline (Durable Functions)
description:
Durable Functions orchestrator that takes uploaded audio through the full pipeline:

1. Transcribe via Azure AI Speech fast transcription API
2. Audio analysis via Parselmouth (pitch, volume, pauses)
   (Steps 1 and 2 run in parallel — neither depends on the other)

3. Fan-out 5 parallel passes (all need transcript; Pass 3 also needs Parselmouth data):
   - Pass 1: Biblical Analysis (o4-mini) — Biblical Accuracy, Time in the Word, Passage Focus
   - Pass 2: Structure & Content (GPT-4.1) — Clarity, Application, Engagement
   - Pass 3: Delivery (GPT-4.1-mini) — Delivery, Emotional Range
   - Sermon type classification + metadata extraction (GPT-4.1-mini) — expository/topical/survey, title, pastor name, main passage
   - Segment classification (GPT-4.1-mini) — label transcript segments as scripture/teaching/application/anecdote/illustration/prayer/transition (required for frontend transcript viewer color-coding)

4. Fan-in

5. Score normalization by sermon type (pure code, no LLM) — needs sermon type from classification

6. Store results in Cosmos DB, update status to complete

## Parselmouth Hosting

Parselmouth is a single 36MB .so with standard libc deps (no exotic native binaries). It CAN run on Azure Functions Linux consumption plan — the sandbox supports standard C++ libs. The 36MB adds to cold start but is within the 1.5GB memory limit. For MVP, deploy Parselmouth as part of the Function App package. If cold starts become a problem, move to a dedicated activity function on Premium plan later.

## Parselmouth Noise Floor Filtering (POC #8)

POC #8 (Scheer sermon) reported 384 dB intensity range — physically impossible. Caused by near-silence frames in the MP3. Fix: filter out frames below a noise floor (bottom 5th percentile of intensity values) before computing intensity stats. Pitch already filters unvoiced frames (pv > 0); apply the same pattern to intensity. Expected real range: 30-80 dB.

## Sermon Type Classification Fix (POC #8)

POC #8 misclassified Scheer's verse-by-verse 1 Peter 1:1-2 sermon as topical (85%) because the classifier only saw the first 2000-3000 chars — which was all intro anecdote. This inflated the composite by ~3 points via normalization bumps.

Fix: send more transcript context to the classifier — first 1500 + middle 1500 + last 1500 chars instead of just first 2000-3000. Alternatively, add a post-classification sanity check: if Pass 1 detects a clear main passage with >40% focus AND scripture density >0.5 refs/min, override topical→expository.

## Error/Retry Strategy

Durable Functions built-in retry policies:
- Transcription (fast API): retry 2x, 30s backoff. If permanent fail → status: failed, error: transcription_error
- Parselmouth audio analysis: retry 1x. If fail → proceed WITHOUT audio metrics, flag delivery/emotional_range scores as partial (audio_metrics: null). Graceful degradation.
- Each LLM pass (1-3): retry 3x, exponential backoff starting at 60s (not 10s — POC #8 showed 429s need 60-180s waits on S0 tier). Stagger parallel pass launches by 2-3s to avoid simultaneous token bursts against the shared TPM quota.
- Classification + metadata: retry 2x, 60s backoff. If fail → default to type: unknown, title: filename, pastor: unknown
- Segment classification: retry 1x. If fail → store transcript without segment labels, frontend shows unsegmented text
- Overall orchestrator timeout: 10 minutes
- On any permanent failure: update Cosmos DB status to failed with error message and failed_at timestamp

Note: S0 Standard tier rate limits (50-80K TPM) are tight for longer sermons. An 8K-word transcript (normal for 45-50 min sermons) with 3-4 parallel passes easily hits the ceiling. POC #8 confirmed this is not an edge case. If rate limits become a persistent problem, options: (1) request quota increase, (2) stagger passes sequentially instead of parallel (adds ~30s latency), (3) upgrade to higher tier.

## WPM Sanity Check

After transcription, check WPM. If < 80 or > 200, flag as suspect but continue processing. Store wpm_flag: true in results so frontend can show a warning.

## Upload Retry

No resume mechanism for failed uploads in MVP. If upload fails mid-transfer (network drop), user must retry from scratch. Frontend shows clear error with retry option. Phase 1: consider chunked upload or direct-to-blob via SAS token.

## Depends On

Infra (sermon-1a3) being deployed. POC #7 validates the full pipeline produces accurate scores on complete transcripts. POC #8 validates pipeline works across different preachers and sermon lengths (8K words / 48 min).

## Cost Target

~$0.75 per sermon ($0.67 speech + $0.09 OpenAI). POC #7 confirmed $0.09 OpenAI on 3,762-word transcript. POC #8 confirmed $0.76 on 8,192-word transcript.

title: Research: DTS sermon evaluation framework alignment with PSR scoring
description:
Pure research — DTS (Dallas Theological Seminary) sermon evaluation criteria and how they compare to PSR.

## DTS Framework: BACL (Biblical, Authentic, Contextual, Life-Changing)

DTS Pastoral Ministries (PM5103/PM5104) grades student sermons on four pillars:

### 1. Biblical (text faithfulness)
- Clear "Big Idea" derived from passage (Haddon Robinson methodology)
- Exegetical idea accurately drawn from text
- Supporting scriptures used in context, not proof-texted
- Clear MPS (Main Point Statement) that whole sermon develops

### 2. Authentic (preacher credibility)
- Personal engagement with text — has it worked on the preacher first?
- Genuine conviction vs performance
- Appropriate vulnerability without self-indulgence

### 3. Contextual (audience connection)
- Bridges ancient world to modern world
- Illustrations from listeners actual lives
- Awareness of congregation circumstances
- Accessible language without dumbing down

### 4. Life-Changing (call to response)
- Clear, actionable applications
- Beyond information to transformation
- Specific call to action — what to do Monday morning

### Delivery mechanics (also graded)
- Voice inflection, volume, clarity
- Eye contact, pulpit presence
- Pacing, transitions, sermon structure
- Illustration quality, time management

## Haddon Robinson Influence
Robinson (DTS faculty, then Gordon-Conwell) — "a sermon should be a bullet, not buckshot." Every sermon needs ONE dominant idea: exegetical idea (what text says) transformed into homiletical idea (what preacher says). DTS grades heavily on this.

## Gaps in PSR vs DTS

### Currently missing from PSR:
1. **Thesis Clarity / Big Idea** — Passage Focus measures time-on-passage, not idea unity. A sermon can spend 90% on one passage and still be scattered. DTS would want: does the sermon have ONE articulable main point?
2. **Theological Depth** — Time in the Word measures density but not depth. DTS distinguishes surface reading from actual theological engagement (word studies, doctrinal connections, systematic theology).
3. **Transformational Intent** — Application category is general. DTS wants specific: does the listener leave with a concrete action item?
4. **Preacher Authenticity** — Hardest to model. DTS cares if the text has personally transformed the preacher. LLM can detect some signals (first-person vulnerability, personal stories tied to text) but cant truly assess genuineness.

### LLM detectability:
- Thesis Clarity: YES — can analyze if sermon has single unified theme
- Theological Depth: YES — can measure doctrinal sophistication, word studies, systematic connections
- Transformational Intent: YES — can detect specific vs vague application
- Preacher Authenticity: PARTIAL — heuristics possible but always approximate

## Sources
- DTS PM5103/PM5104 course descriptions (dts.edu)
- DTS sermon evaluation form (BACL framework, Scribd doc 344742047)
- Haddon Robinson "Biblical Preaching" methodology
- Dr. Tim White sermon evaluation form (Textual Faithfulness, Instruction, Communication, Delivery)
- Truett Seminary survey on dimensions of effective preaching (Notre Dame Church Life Journal)
- DTS Hendricks Center podcast on Bible exposition with Carl Laney

## Decision needed
Whether to add DTS-aligned metrics to PSR scoring, and if so, which ones. Thesis Clarity and Theological Depth are the strongest candidates — both are LLM-detectable and address real gaps.

title: Document DNS setup research (howwas.church → Azure SWA)
description:
## Summary

Documented the full DNS configuration process for howwas.church → Azure Static Web Apps in docs/AZURE_SETUP.md.

## What Was Learned

### Porkbun API
- All requests are POST (even reads/deletes) — unusual but consistent
- Auth via apikey + secretapikey in every request body
- API access must be explicitly enabled per-domain in the Porkbun dashboard (off by default)
- Base URL: https://api.porkbun.com/api/json/v3
- New domains come with default parking records (ALIAS + wildcard CNAME to pixie.porkbun.com) — delete these before adding real records
- Porkbun routes through Cloudflare by default — this is fine but means dig CNAME on the apex returns empty (resolved behind proxy as A record)

### Azure Static Web Apps Custom Domains
- Subdomains (www): CNAME validation — automatic, takes ~2 minutes
- Apex domains (root): Cannot use CNAME validation. Must use --validation-method dns-txt-token:
  1. az staticwebapp hostname set --hostname howwas.church --validation-method dns-txt-token --no-wait
  2. az staticwebapp hostname show --hostname howwas.church --query validationToken to get the token
  3. Add TXT record via Porkbun API
  4. Wait for Azure to validate (took ~15 min for us, docs say up to 10 min)
  5. Delete TXT record after validation completes
- SSL certificates are auto-provisioned and auto-renewed by Azure — no manual cert management
- HSTS is enabled by default on SWA custom domains

### Timing
- DNS propagation (Porkbun to public resolvers): < 1 minute (600s TTL but Cloudflare is fast)
- Azure www validation: ~2 minutes
- Azure apex TXT validation: ~15 minutes
- Total process: ~20 minutes end-to-end

## Where It Is Documented
- docs/AZURE_SETUP.md — Domain section, DNS Setup subsection
- Includes active records, setup steps, gotchas, useful commands, and Porkbun API reference
close_reason: Research documented in docs/AZURE_SETUP.md under DNS Setup section. Covers Porkbun API usage, Azure SWA apex vs subdomain validation, timing expectations, gotchas (Cloudflare proxy, TXT validation), and useful commands.

title: Radar chart axis labels clipped on mobile viewports
description:
## Problem

On mobile viewports (~375px), the Recharts RadarChart axis labels are clipped by the container boundary. "Delivery" renders as "elivery", "Passage Focus" as "Passa". The labels overflow the `max-w-[400px] aspect-square` container div.

## Research

### Current implementation (web/src/components/RadarView.tsx)
- Uses `ResponsiveContainer` wrapping a `RadarChart`
- Container: `div.w-full.max-w-[400px].aspect-square`
- `PolarAngleAxis` tick: `{ fontSize: 11, fill: "#6b7280" }`
- Labels already abbreviated: "Biblical Acc.", "Emot. Range", "Time in Word"
- But "Passage Focus", "Engagement", "Application", "Delivery", "Clarity" are full-length

### Known Recharts issue
This is a known limitation — recharts/recharts#2450 (opened Feb 2021, still open). `ResponsiveContainer` does not auto-shrink the radar polygon to make room for labels. The labels are positioned at the polygon edge and overflow the SVG viewBox.

### Fix options

**Option A: Reduce outerRadius on small screens (recommended)**
Recharts `RadarChart` accepts an `outerRadius` prop. Default is auto-calculated to fill the container. Setting it to ~80% leaves room for labels. Can use a `useMediaQuery` or just set a fixed `outerRadius` like 120 that works at all sizes. Tradeoff: slightly smaller chart on desktop.

**Option B: Custom tick renderer with dynamic positioning**
Use `PolarAngleAxis tick={renderCustomTick}` where the custom tick pushes labels further from center using `(y - cy) / 2` offset (per SO answer). More complex but preserves chart size.

**Option C: Shorter labels on mobile**
Abbreviate all labels to 3-4 chars on small screens: "Bib. Acc.", "Word", "Focus", "Clarity", "Engage", "Apply", "Deliv.", "Emot.". Use a media query or container query. Simple but loses readability.

**Option D: Smaller font on mobile**
Drop `fontSize` from 11 to 9 on small screens. Quick fix but may hurt readability.

### Recommendation
Option A (reduce outerRadius) is simplest and most robust. Set `outerRadius={110}` or similar — this leaves ~30px padding on each side for labels within the 400px container. Can combine with Option C for extra safety on very small screens.

### Spec reference
frontend-spec.md says "Fixed size: ~400×400px, centered" and "Axis labels are category names (abbreviated if needed)". The spec anticipated this — abbreviation is explicitly allowed.
close_reason: Already fixed in commit 8700607 — outerRadius set to 65%, labels wrapped to multi-line, container widened to 480px. Labels no longer clip on mobile.

title: SWA routing breaks sermon list page and causes 'Sermon not found' on direct /sermons access
description:
## Problem

User constantly sees 'Sermon not found.' when accessing howwas.church and navigating to sermons.

## Root Cause — SWA Rewrite Rule Collision

The `staticwebapp.config.json` has this rewrite rule:

```json
{"route": "/sermons/*", "rewrite": "/sermons/placeholder.html"}
```

This rule is intended to route dynamic sermon detail URLs (`/sermons/<uuid>`) to the static export's placeholder page. However, Azure SWA's wildcard matching also catches:

- `/sermons` (the list page) — rewrites to `/sermons/placeholder.html` instead of serving `sermons.html`
- `/sermons/` (trailing slash) — same problem

The Next.js static export produces two separate files:
- `out/sermons.html` — the sermon list page (SermonsPage component, 960px width, shows table of sermons)
- `out/sermons/placeholder.html` — the sermon detail page (SermonDetailClient component, 720px width)

Because the rewrite catches `/sermons`, the list page HTML is never served on direct page load.

## Failure Chain

### Path 1: Direct access to /sermons (list page)
1. User navigates to `howwas.church/sermons`
2. SWA rewrite `/sermons/*` matches → serves `sermons/placeholder.html` (detail page)
3. SermonDetailClient mounts, first useEffect runs
4. `window.location.pathname.split('/').pop()` returns `'sermons'` (not a UUID, not 'placeholder')
5. `setId('sermons')` — the string 'sermons' is used as the sermon ID
6. API call: `GET /api/sermons/sermons` → Cosmos DB lookup for id='sermons' → 404
7. User sees 'Sermon not found.'

### Path 2: Direct access to /sermons/ (trailing slash)
1. `window.location.pathname.split('/').pop()` returns `''` (empty string)
2. Empty string is falsy → falls through to `params.id !== 'placeholder'` check
3. `params.id` IS 'placeholder' (baked into RSC payload) → `setId(null)`
4. Second useEffect has `if (!id) return` — never fetches, never sets loading=false
5. User sees 'Loading...' forever

### Path 3: Client-side navigation works fine
When navigating from upload page → sermons list via `<Link href='/sermons'>`, Next.js fetches `/sermons.txt` (RSC flight data at root level, not caught by the rewrite). The list page renders correctly. Clicking a sermon row → `router.push(/sermons/<uuid>)` also works because the RSC flight data is fetched from `/sermons/<uuid>.txt` (rewritten to `/sermons/placeholder.txt` correctly) and `window.location.pathname` has the real UUID.

### Path 4: Cold start amplifies the problem
Azure Functions Consumption plan deallocates after ~20 min idle. First request after cold start gets TCP connection refused. The `fetchWithRetry()` in SermonDetailClient retries 3 times with 2s/4s/6s backoff for network errors, but if the Function App returns a non-404 error during startup (e.g., 500 from Key Vault ref resolution delay), it's treated as a server error and shown immediately without retry.

## Verified via Testing

- `curl https://howwas.church/sermons` → serves detail page HTML (720px Shell with 'Loading...')
- `curl https://howwas.church/sermons/` → same
- `curl https://howwas.church/sermons/<valid-uuid>` → serves detail page HTML (correct, JS resolves ID from URL)
- `curl https://psr-functions-dev.azurewebsites.net/api/sermons` → 200, returns 6 sermons
- `curl https://psr-functions-dev.azurewebsites.net/api/sermons/<valid-uuid>` → 200
- `curl https://psr-functions-dev.azurewebsites.net/api/sermons/sermons` → would return 404
- CORS headers present and correct for `Origin: https://howwas.church`
- API_BASE correctly baked into JS: `https://psr-functions-dev.azurewebsites.net`
- All 6 sermons in Cosmos DB are status=complete and accessible via API
- 2 of 6 API calls returned HTTP 000 (connection refused) on first attempt, succeeded on retry — confirms cold start / transient failures

## Fix Options

### Option A: Fix the SWA rewrite to not match /sermons (recommended)
Change the rewrite rule to only match paths with a segment after /sermons/:

```json
{"route": "/sermons/:id", "rewrite": "/sermons/placeholder.html"}
```

SWA named parameters (`:id`) match exactly one path segment, so `/sermons` won't match but `/sermons/<uuid>` will. Same for the .txt rule:

```json
{"route": "/sermons/:id.txt", "rewrite": "/sermons/placeholder.txt"}
```

If SWA doesn't support named params in routes, alternative:
- Remove the `/sermons/*` rewrite entirely and rely on `navigationFallback` to serve `/index.html` for unknown paths. But this would serve the upload page instead of the detail page — the detail page JS chunk would still load via client-side routing, but there'd be a flash of the upload page first.

### Option B: Harden the ID resolution in SermonDetailClient
Add a UUID format check so 'sermons' (or any non-UUID string) is rejected:

```typescript
const UUID_RE = /^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}$/i;
useEffect(() => {
  const seg = window.location.pathname.split('/').pop() || '';
  const resolved = UUID_RE.test(seg) ? seg : UUID_RE.test(params.id) ? params.id : null;
  setId(resolved);
}, [params.id]);
```

This prevents the 'sermons' string from being used as an ID, but doesn't fix the underlying routing problem (list page still won't load on direct access).

### Recommended: Both A and B
Fix the SWA routing (A) so the list page is served correctly, AND add the UUID guard (B) as defense-in-depth so the detail page gracefully handles bad IDs instead of making a doomed API call.

## Related
- sermon-cuo (closed): Fixed NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL not being set — same deploy.sh, different symptom
- sermonplan.md 'Known Issues (Frontend)': Documents the cold start + hydration race, but not this SWA routing collision
- The `navigationFallback` in staticwebapp.config.json excludes `/api/*` and `/_next/*` but doesn't account for the route rule precedence over static files
close_reason: Fixed. Two changes: (1) Added exact route rule for /sermons → /sermons.html in staticwebapp.config.json, placed before the /sermons/* wildcard so the list page is served correctly on direct access. (2) Added UUID format guard in SermonDetailClient so non-UUID path segments (like 'sermons') are rejected gracefully instead of making a doomed API call. Deployed and verified: /sermons serves list page (6838 bytes), /sermons/ serves list page, /sermons/<uuid> serves detail page (6704 bytes), RSC flight data for client-side nav works, API CORS works.

title: LLM-based bonus metrics (Hebrew/Greek/Aramaic + Church History) via Pass 4
description:
Replace client-side regex detection of Hebrew/Greek/Aramaic references and Church History references with LLM-based analysis during the scoring pipeline.

## Current State
- Regex-based, runs client-side on admin page
- ~70% accurate — misses indirect references (e.g. 'the Reformer from Wittenberg'), false positives on common names (e.g. 'my friend Calvin')

## Proposed: Add Pass 4 to scoring pipeline
- New activity: pass4_enrichment running in parallel with passes 1-3
- Model: gpt-5-nano (~$0.005/sermon)
- Zero risk to existing scores — other passes unchanged

## Pass 4 would detect:
1. **Biblical language references** — Hebrew, Greek, Aramaic words/roots/translations, even when language isn't explicitly named (e.g. 'hesed', 'agape')
2. **Church history references** — historical figures, events, councils, creeds, eras (non-biblical)

## Output stored in Cosmos DB:
```json
{
  "enrichment": {
    "biblicalLanguages": { "count": 5, "references": [{"quote": "...", "language": "Greek", "term": "agape"}] },
    "churchHistory": { "count": 3, "references": [{"quote": "...", "figure_or_event": "Luther", "era": "Reformation"}] }
  }
}
```

## Admin page changes:
- Read pre-computed counts from DB instead of running regex
- Counts are read-only (LLM-determined), bonus slider still manual
- Remove client-side regex scanning code

## Benefits:
- ~95%+ accuracy vs ~70% with regex
- Understands context (indirect references, disambiguation)
- Runs once at scoring time, not on every admin page load
- Easy to extend later (illustration tracking, theological vocabulary, etc.)
close_reason: Implemented pass4_enrichment activity (gpt-5-nano) for LLM-based biblical language + church history detection. Wired into both audio and text orchestrators in parallel with existing passes. Admin page now reads pre-computed counts from API instead of running client-side regex. Enrichment stored in Cosmos DB. Rescore also updated. Pipeline version bumped to 2026-03-09a.

title: LLM-based Illustration Tracker (upgrade from regex to Pass 4)
description:
Upgrade the Illustration Tracker on the pastor dashboard from client-side regex to LLM-based classification via Pass 4.

## Current State (Researched 2026-03-09)

### Client-side regex (dashboard/page.tsx lines 260-277)
Runs on every dashboard page load, scanning transcript text with regex:
- **Personal Story**: matches "i remember", "my wife", "when i was", etc. (19 patterns)
- **Historical**: matches "in the Nth century", "reformation", "world war", etc. (12 patterns)
- **Hypothetical**: matches "imagine", "what if", "suppose", etc. (10 patterns)
- **Humor**: matches "laugh", "funny", "joke", etc. (9 patterns)

Problems:
- ~60-70% accurate — misses subtle illustrations, cant distinguish a joke from mentioning the word "funny"
- Runs on every page load (wasteful, slow on large transcripts)
- Regex cant understand context (e.g. "I remember reading in Romans" is not a personal story)

### Pass 4 already exists (activities.py line 375)
- `pass4_enrichment()` already runs in parallel with passes 1-3 in the orchestrator
- Currently handles: biblicalLanguages + churchHistory
- Uses gpt-5-nano, output stored in Cosmos DB under `enrichment` field
- Called from both audio and text orchestrators + rescore

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Extend Pass 4 prompt (activities.py)
Add a third section to the pass4_enrichment prompt:

```
3. **Illustrations** — Classify each illustration/anecdote/example used:
   - Personal Story: speaker shares from their own life experience
   - Historical: references to historical events, figures, or eras (non-biblical)
   - Hypothetical: "imagine if...", thought experiments, scenarios
   - Humor: jokes, funny stories, comedic moments
   Return each with a short quote and classification.
```

Output addition:
```json
{
  "enrichment": {
    "biblicalLanguages": {...},
    "churchHistory": {...},
    "illustrations": {
      "total": 8,
      "byType": {
        "personalStory": [{"quote": "...", "context": "..."}],
        "historical": [{"quote": "...", "context": "..."}],
        "hypothetical": [{"quote": "...", "context": "..."}],
        "humor": [{"quote": "...", "context": "..."}]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### 2. Update schema validation (schema.py)
Add `illustrations` to the enrichment validation defaults.

### 3. Update dashboard (web/src/app/dashboard/page.tsx)
- Remove regex scanning block (lines 260-277)
- Read illustration counts from `sermon.enrichment.illustrations.byType`
- Pie chart reads from stored counts instead of computing on the fly
- Fallback: if enrichment.illustrations is missing (old sermons), show "Rescore to enable"

### 4. Rescore path (activities.py rescore function)
Already calls pass4_enrichment — will automatically pick up the new prompt.

### 5. Existing sermons
Old sermons wont have illustration data until rescored. Dashboard should handle gracefully.

## Cost Impact
- gpt-5-nano prompt grows ~200 tokens — negligible cost increase (~$0.001/sermon)
- Removes client-side computation on every dashboard load

## Blocker
- sermon-2qb (Pass 4 LLM-based bonus metrics) — CLOSED, already implemented

## Testing
- Rescore one sermon, verify enrichment.illustrations appears in Cosmos DB
- Dashboard pie chart should show LLM-classified counts
- Compare LLM vs old regex counts on a known sermon (expect higher accuracy)

title: Smart rescore: selective passes, parallel fan-out, and Batch API
description:
Reduce rescore time and cost as sermon count grows. Three strategies, implementable independently.

## Problem
Full rescore of 33 sermons took 90 minutes and ~$1.50. At scale:
- 100 sermons: ~4.5 hours, ~$1.67
- 500 sermons: ~16.7 hours, ~$8.33
- 1,000 sermons: ~33 hours, ~$16.66

Time is the bottleneck, not cost. Sequential processing (1 sermon at a time) is the root cause.

## Current Cost Breakdown Per Sermon (~$0.017)
- Pass 1 (biblical/o4-mini): $0.011 — 67% of total cost
- Pass 2 (structure/gpt-5-mini): $0.003
- Pass 3 (delivery/gpt-5-nano): $0.001
- Pass 4 (enrichment/gpt-5-nano): $0.001
- Classify + Segments + Summary (gpt-5-nano): $0.001

## Strategy 1: Selective Per-Pass Rescore (HIGH priority)
When: You only changed one pass prompt (e.g. Pass 4 enrichment)
Savings: Pass 4-only rescore = $0.001/sermon (98% reduction). Time drops proportionally.

Add passes parameter to rescore endpoint:
- POST /api/rescore {"passes": [4], "all": true} — Re-run enrichment only
- POST /api/rescore {"passes": [1,2,3], "all": true} — Re-run scoring only
- POST /api/rescore {"passes": ["segments"], "all": true} — Re-classify segments only
- POST /api/rescore {"all": true} — Full rescore (current behavior)

Each pass stores its own promptVersion hash in Cosmos DB. Composite score recalculated only if passes 1-3 are included.

## Strategy 2: Parallel Fan-Out Rescore (MEDIUM priority)
When: Full rescore needed but 100+ sermons
Savings: 1,000 sermons in ~10-15 min instead of 33 hours

Replace sequential loop in rescore_orchestrator with Durable Functions fan-out/fan-in. Fire N sermons as parallel activities, throttle batches to stay within Azure OpenAI TPM limits. o4-mini S0 tier at 80K TPM supports ~10 sermons in parallel.

## Strategy 3: Azure OpenAI Batch API (MEDIUM-LOW, Phase 1)
When: 500+ sermons, non-urgent rescores (can wait up to 24 hours)
Savings: 50% cost reduction on all API calls, separate quota

Key findings: Azure OpenAI Global Batch API offers 50% cost reduction with 24-hour turnaround. Available in eastus2 for o4-mini and gpt-5-nano. Separate enqueued token quota wont affect live scoring. Requires GlobalBatch deployment type. Max 100K requests per file.

## Strategy 4: Score Versioning + Lazy Rescore (LOW, Phase 1+)
When: Frequent prompt tweaks, large sermon library
Store promptVersionHash per sermon. On page load, compare vs current. Show old score with badge if mismatch. User clicks to rescore on-demand. Zero bulk rescore cost.

## Recommended Order
1. Strategy 1 (selective passes) — immediate
2. Strategy 2 (parallel fan-out) — at ~100 sermons
3. Strategy 3 (Batch API) — Phase 1
4. Strategy 4 (lazy rescore) — Phase 1+

title: Verify yellow/red score colors render correctly with sub-70 scores
description:
## Problem

Every sermon scored so far (Piper 82, POC sermons 72-92) has all-green scores (70+). The yellow (50-69) and red (<50) color paths in the UI have never been visually verified on the live site.

## Research

### Color logic (web/src/lib/types.ts)
The `scoreColor` and `scoreBgColor` functions implement the three-tier coloring:
- Green (70+): `text-green-500` / `bg-green-500`
- Yellow (50-69): `text-yellow-500` / `bg-yellow-500`
- Red (<50): `text-red-500` / `bg-red-500`

These are used in:
- Feed page PSR column (large bold number)
- Score gauge arc color
- Category card progress bars
- Category card score numbers

### What could go wrong
1. **Tailwind purging**: If `text-yellow-500`, `bg-yellow-500`, `text-red-500`, `bg-red-500` are never used in the pre-rendered HTML, Tailwind's JIT compiler might not include them in the CSS bundle. They're only referenced via dynamic template literals (`scoreColor(score)` returns a class string), which Tailwind can't statically analyze.
2. **Score gauge SVG**: The arc color is computed from the score — need to verify the SVG stroke changes color at the 70 and 50 thresholds.
3. **Radar chart**: Single blue fill regardless of score — no color issue here.

### Tailwind purge risk assessment
Checked the Tailwind docs: JIT mode scans source files for class names. Since `scoreColor` returns full class strings like `"text-green-500"`, `"text-yellow-500"`, `"text-red-500"` as complete string literals in the types.ts file, Tailwind WILL detect them during scanning. So purging should NOT be an issue — the classes are present as complete strings in the source.

However, `scoreBgColor` returns `"bg-green-500"` etc. — same pattern, should also be safe.

### Verification plan
This is blocked by sermon-e25 (mediocre sermon benchmark). Once a sub-70 sermon is scored, visually verify:
1. Feed page shows yellow/red PSR number
2. Score gauge arc is yellow/red
3. Category bars with sub-70 scores show yellow, sub-50 show red
4. No missing CSS classes (Tailwind purge)

### Dependency
Blocked by sermon-e25 (need a sermon that actually scores in the yellow/red range).
close_reason: Verified via automated browser tests + visual screenshot inspection. All 3 color tiers render correctly: green (70+), yellow (50-69), red (<50). Tested on list page (17 sermons), yellow detail (62.2), red detail (23.2), green detail (88.4). Gauge arcs, score numbers, and category progress bars all use correct colors. No code changes needed.

title: Strengths/improvements are generic category restatements, not sermon-specific
description:
## Problem

The strengths and improvements displayed on the detail page are generic restatements of category names rather than specific observations about the sermon content.

Actual output for Piper "Called According to His Purpose" (PSR 82):
- Strengths: "High biblical accuracy", "Clear passage focus", "Effective delivery"
- Improvements: "Enhance application relevance", "Increase emotional range", "Boost listener engagement"

These could apply to any sermon scoring similarly. Compare to the category reasoning text which IS specific: "All cited passages are accurately quoted... The preacher distinguishes properly between general and effectual calling using Romans and 1 Corinthians."

## Root cause

The `build_summary_prompt` function in `api/schema.py` (line 68-76) only passes category names and scores to the LLM:

```
Given these sermon scores (type: expository):
- biblicalAccuracy: 90/100
- timeInTheWord: 80/100
...
Return JSON: summary, strengths (3), improvements (2-3)
```

No reasoning text, no transcript excerpts, no sermon-specific context. The LLM has nothing to work with except numbers and category names, so it can only produce generic output.

## Fix options

**Option A: Include reasoning text in the summary prompt (recommended)**
Pass each category's reasoning string into `build_summary_prompt`. The reasoning already contains sermon-specific observations (scripture refs, illustration names, structural analysis). This gives the LLM enough context to generate specific strengths like "Effective use of the construction foundation analogy to explain Romans 8:28-30" instead of "Clear passage focus".

Cost impact: minimal — the reasoning strings total ~500-800 tokens, and this is already a gpt-41-mini call.

**Option B: Include transcript excerpts**
Pass the first/last 500 words of transcript. More context but higher token cost and the reasoning text already distills the relevant observations.

**Option C: Two-pass approach**
First generate reasoning per category (already done), then generate summary from reasoning (Option A). This is effectively what Option A does without adding a pass.

### Recommendation
Option A. Change `build_summary_prompt` to include the reasoning field from each category. The prompt becomes:

```
Given these sermon scores (type: expository):
- biblicalAccuracy: 90/100 — "All cited passages are accurately quoted..."
- timeInTheWord: 80/100 — "Roughly four-fifths of the sermon..."
...
```

Expected output: "Strong exegesis of Romans 8:28-30 with effective construction foundation analogy; application could move beyond doctrinal implications to concrete daily scenarios."

### Files to change
- `api/schema.py`: `build_summary_prompt()` — include reasoning in the prompt template
- `api/activities.py`: `generate_summary()` — already passes categories dict which contains reasoning, just need the prompt to use it
close_reason: Fixed build_summary_prompt in schema.py to include category reasoning text in the prompt. The LLM now has sermon-specific observations (scripture refs, structural analysis, illustration names) to generate specific strengths/improvements instead of generic category restatements. Added test_contains_reasoning test. Deployed to production. Existing sermons retain old summaries; new uploads will get specific output.

title: Choose vanity domain name for PSR
description:
## Domain Name Research (RDAP-verified 2026-03-02)

## VERIFIED AVAILABLE — Creative TLDs

title: Add pipelineVersion tracking + batch rescore capability (admin-only)
description:
## Problem

No way to know which models scored a sermon. When models change (like the gpt-4.1 → gpt-5-mini swap), old scores become silently stale. No mechanism to re-score existing sermons with new models.

## Part 1: Pipeline Version Tracking

Add a `pipelineVersion` field to every sermon document stored in Cosmos DB.

### Schema

```json
{
  "pipelineVersion": "2026-03-06",
  "scoringModels": {
    "pass1_biblical": "o4-mini",
    "pass2_structure": "gpt-5-mini",
    "pass3_delivery": "gpt-5-nano",
    "classification": "gpt-5-nano"
  }
}
```

### Implementation

One-line change in `function_app.py` orchestrator — add `pipelineVersion` and `scoringModels` to the `updates` dict (~line 327). Read model names from a config constant so they stay in sync with `activities.py`.

### Existing sermons

The 4 sermons already in Cosmos DB have no `pipelineVersion`. They were scored with:
- Pass 1: o4-mini (unchanged)
- Pass 2: gpt-41 (GPT-4.1)
- Pass 3: gpt-41-mini (GPT-4.1-mini)

Options: (a) backfill with a one-time script, or (b) leave null — frontend treats null as "legacy/unknown".

## Part 2: Batch Rescore (Admin-Only, NOT User-Facing)

Re-run LLM scoring passes on existing transcripts without re-transcribing. No UI button — this is an admin/CLI operation only.

### Research: Approaches

**Option A: Azure Functions HTTP endpoint with admin key**
- New endpoint: `POST /api/admin/rescore` with function-level auth key
- Accepts: `{"sermonIds": ["id1", "id2"]}` or `{"all": true, "olderThan": "2026-03-06"}`
- For each sermon: read transcript from Cosmos → re-run 3 LLM passes + classification + normalization → update document
- Skips transcription ($0.67) and Parselmouth (already stored as audioMetrics) — cost is ~$0.04/sermon
- Pros: simple, uses existing infra, can be called via curl
- Cons: 10-min Azure Functions timeout could be tight for large batches

**Option B: Durable Functions orchestrator for rescore**
- New orchestrator: `rescore_orchestrator` that fans out across sermons
- Each sermon is an activity that reads transcript + audioMetrics from Cosmos, re-runs LLM passes, updates doc
- Pros: no timeout issues, parallel processing, retry built in
- Cons: more code, but reuses existing activity functions

**Option C: Local Python script**
- Script in `scripts/rescore.py` that reads from Cosmos, calls Azure OpenAI directly, writes back
- Pros: simplest, no deployment needed, full control
- Cons: runs on your machine, needs env vars, no retry/monitoring

### Recommendation

**Option B (Durable Functions orchestrator)** for production use. It reuses the existing activity functions (pass1_biblical, pass2_structure, pass3_delivery, etc.) so scoring logic stays DRY. The fan-out pattern handles any number of sermons without timeout. Trigger it via a simple admin HTTP endpoint with function key auth.

For MVP volume (<50 sermons), **Option C (local script)** is also fine as a stopgap.

### Cost Analysis

| Sermons | LLM-only rescore cost | Full re-process cost |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 4 (current) | $0.16 | $3.00 |
| 10 | $0.40 | $7.50 |
| 50 | $2.00 | $37.50 |
| 100 | $4.00 | $75.00 |

Rescore is 95% cheaper than full re-process because it skips transcription ($0.67/sermon).

### When to rescore

- After a model upgrade (like gpt-4.1 → gpt-5-mini)
- After a prompt change that affects scoring (like the Time in the Word redefinition in POC #10)
- After normalization curve recalibration (bead sermon-4k1)
- NOT on every deploy — only when scoring logic or models change

### Security

- No public-facing rescore button. Users should not trigger rescoring.
- Admin endpoint protected by Azure Functions host key or function key
- Could also be triggered via Azure CLI: `func azure functionapp publish ... && curl -X POST .../api/admin/rescore?code=FUNCTION_KEY`

### Data Safety

- Always preserve the original scores in a `previousScores` array before overwriting
- Store `rescoredAt` timestamp and `rescoredFrom` (old pipelineVersion)
- This creates an audit trail: you can see how scores changed across model generations
close_reason: Implemented both parts. (1) Every sermon now stores pipelineVersion + scoringModels in Cosmos DB. (2) Admin-only rescore: POST /api/admin/rescore with function key auth. Durable Functions rescore_orchestrator fans out, re-runs 3 LLM passes + classification on existing transcripts, preserves old scores in previousScores array. ~$0.04/sermon.
